stories.title: The Ship That Launched a Class
stories.intended_reader: Cruise enthusiasts interested in fleet history
stories.core_insight: Statendam introduced the S-Class design that defined 1990s HAL
stories.markdown:
**I was aboard [Statendam](/ships/holland-america-line/statendam.html) on her very first Alaska voyage in 1993, and I remember the moment I stepped into the atrium and understood that Holland America had just reinvented itself—the space rose through multiple decks in a cascade of light and glass that felt less like a ship and more like a cathedral dedicated to the art of cruising.**

Statendam was first. Everything the S-Class became, she pioneered. At 1,266 passengers, she struck a balance between the intimacy of the old HAL liners and the amenity expectations of a new generation of cruise travelers who wanted more than cabin and dining room.

I had sailed Holland America ships for twenty years before Statendam arrived, watching the line evolve from its ocean liner heritage into an uncertain future. The older ships were elegant but aging. The competition was building bigger, flashier, more spectacular. Holland America needed to respond without abandoning the traditions that defined it.

Statendam was that response. She introduced the atrium design that would become an S-Class signature—an open, light-filled center that made the ship feel larger than her tonnage suggested. She debuted the specialty dining concepts that would transform HAL's culinary identity. She carried an art collection featuring Dutch masters that treated the ship as gallery and the passengers as patrons.

The design choices were deliberate. Statendam was not chasing the mega-ship trend that competitors were pursuing. She was forging a middle path—intimate enough for personal service, large enough for modern amenities. The balance she struck would define Holland America for the next two decades.

Four sister ships followed, each refining what Statendam had pioneered. But she was first. She carried the risk. She proved the concept. Every S-Class ship that followed owed its existence to the gamble that Statendam represented.

I sailed her again in 2010, near the end of her HAL career. She had aged, as ships do. The atrium that had stunned me in 1993 now seemed modest compared to what newer ships offered. The dining concepts she had pioneered were now standard across the industry. But her significance had not diminished. She was the original—the ship that proved that Holland America could evolve without abandoning its soul.

Those who sailed Statendam in her early years remember the amazement. The feeling that something important was happening. The ship that launched a class and launched Holland America into its modern era.

**The lesson:** Pioneers are remembered not for perfection but for the courage to go first—and the ships that launch new eras deserve gratitude even after newer ships surpass them.

stories.title: Alaska Before Mega-Ships
stories.core_insight: Statendam delivered Alaska at a scale the industry has largely abandoned
stories.markdown:
**We sailed [Statendam](/ships/holland-america-line/statendam.html) to Alaska in 1995, when cruise ships were still small enough to feel like guests in the wilderness rather than invaders of it, and I remember standing at the rail in Tracy Arm Inlet with only two other passengers beside me, watching ice calve into water so blue it seemed artificial.**

That Alaska no longer exists—not because the glaciers have disappeared, though they are diminished, but because the ships have grown so large that the intimacy Statendam offered is now impossible. In 1995, Statendam carried 1,266 passengers through the Inside Passage. Today, ships carrying five thousand make the same transit.

The difference was not just numerical. It was experiential. Statendam's naturalists had time for questions because there were fewer passengers asking them. The viewing decks never crowded because there were fewer people competing for rail space. The tender operations at Juneau and Ketchikan deposited a manageable number of passengers on docks that could absorb them without strain.

Glacier Bay felt more private. We entered the bay at dawn, with a park ranger aboard who narrated our approach to the Margerie Glacier as if giving a private lecture. The passengers gathered on the forward decks, and everyone—everyone—had a clear view. No jostling. No craning. No disappointment. The glacier existed for us, and we were few enough to experience it individually rather than collectively.

Tracy Arm felt more adventurous. The narrow inlet required careful navigation, and Statendam's size allowed the captain to penetrate deeper than larger ships could attempt. We floated among icebergs close enough to touch—or so it seemed—while seals watched us from their frozen perches with the calm indifference of creatures who had seen ships before but not too many.

The wildlife encounters were different too. Humpback whales surfacing near a ship carrying 1,266 passengers created a moment. Humpbacks near a ship carrying five thousand create a commotion. The quiet that Statendam's scale preserved allowed us to hear the whale breathe—the massive exhalation followed by the drawn-out inhale, then the flick of the tail as it dove. These sounds require silence to hear, and silence requires small ships.

I have returned to Alaska on larger ships since. The scenery is still magnificent. The wildlife is still present. But the intimacy is gone. The Alaska that Statendam showed us existed in a window between the arrival of modern cruising and the arrival of mega-ships—a brief, perfect era when the ships were large enough for comfort and small enough for wonder.

**The lesson:** Some experiences require intimacy that only small scale can provide—and the ships that once offered that scale deserve remembrance for the wilderness they let us truly see.

stories.title: When Statendam Was New
stories.markdown:
**I sailed [Statendam](/ships/holland-america-line/statendam.html) in 1994, her second year of service, when everything aboard still smelled of fresh varnish and new carpet, and I understood immediately that Holland America had built something that would change what premium cruising meant.**

The ship felt modern in ways I had not expected. I had sailed HAL ships since the 1970s—grand old vessels with heritage and atmosphere but increasingly dated facilities. Statendam was different. She felt current, confident, designed for the decade she served rather than the decade that had passed.

The atrium rose through multiple decks, flooding the ship's center with natural light. Other lines had built atriums, but Statendam's felt different—warmer, more proportional, designed to welcome rather than to impress. Dutch design sensibility tempered the spectacle with restraint.

The dining room welcomed single seating—a revolution for passengers who had endured the tyranny of early and late seatings on previous ships. You ate when you wanted. You dressed as the occasion required. The freedom was subtle but transformative, treating passengers as adults capable of managing their own evening schedules.

The spa suggested that cruising could be about wellness—not just eating and entertainment but genuine physical and mental restoration. The concept seems obvious now, but in 1994, the idea that a cruise ship should include a serious fitness and wellness facility was genuinely innovative.

I sat in the Crow's Nest lounge that first evening, watching Alaska's Inside Passage slide past the panoramic windows, and felt something I had not felt aboard a ship in years: surprise. Statendam had surprised me. She had proven that Holland America could evolve—could build something new without abandoning what made the line distinctive.

I have sailed many ships since. But Statendam set expectations that later ships had to match. She defined what premium cruising meant for a generation of travelers who discovered HAL through her S-Class innovations.

The future that became foundation. The surprise that became standard.

**The lesson:** Experience teaches discernment—and the ships that redefine our expectations deserve credit for every expectation they taught us to hold.

stories.title: Discovering Alaska Alone
stories.intended_reader: Solo travelers considering Alaska cruise experiences
stories.core_insight: Statendam's S-Class intimacy and Alaska's transformative landscapes created perfect conditions for solo self-discovery
stories.markdown:
**I booked Alaska solo because I needed to remember who I was before I became a wife, a mother, a career. Statendam gave me the space to find out.**

The travel agent hesitated when I called. 'Alaska alone? Most people go with family.' That was exactly why I needed to go alone. Twenty-three years of defining myself through others. I needed a voyage about me.

[Statendam](/ships/holland-america-line/statendam.html) welcomed solo travelers better than I'd expected. The S-Class ships carry just over a thousand passengers—small enough that faces become familiar, large enough that solitude remains available. I could find company in the Lido or silence in the library.

Alaska itself demanded attention. Standing at the rail watching Glacier Bay, I didn't need conversation. The ice spoke. The humpback whales spoke. The silence between calving events spoke loudest of all. Nature provided company that required no explanation.

The naturalist aboard Statendam adopted me and three other solo travelers as her special students. She taught us to identify bird species, explained glacial geology, stayed up late to watch us spot our first northern lights. The education filled hours that might have felt empty.

I ate dinner at different tables each night—the captain's invitation one evening, solo travelers' table another, a couple from Seattle who'd asked me to join them. The variety energized rather than exhausted. Each meal brought new perspectives.

Ketchikan's totem poles taught me about family identity. Juneau's gold rush history taught me about reinvention. Skagway's Chilkoot Trail taught me about endurance. The destinations educated while the ship restored.

**The rediscovery:** Statendam gave me eleven days to remember myself. Alaska provided the mirrors. The voyage that returned someone I'd lost.

I came home different. More certain of what mattered. More willing to claim time as mine. Statendam started something that continues still.

**The lesson:** Solitude doesn't have to mean isolation.

stories.title: Four Generations See Alaska
stories.core_insight: Statendam's Alaska itinerary created shared experiences across four generations of family
stories.markdown:
**Great-Grandma Ellen turned ninety in Glacier Bay. Four generations of Hendersons celebrated with her.**

Planning this trip required eighteen months of coordination. Great-Grandma Ellen in her wheelchair. My parents in their sixties. My sister and me with our spouses. Six grandchildren ranging from seven to sixteen. Fourteen people, four generations, one [Statendam](/ships/holland-america-line/statendam.html) Alaska voyage.

The logistics nearly defeated us. Accessible cabins for Great-Grandma. Connecting rooms for families with young children. Teenagers who demanded independence. HAL's group coordinator earned our eternal gratitude by solving every puzzle.

The magic happened unexpectedly. Great-Grandma Ellen had seen Alaska before—in 1957, on a steamship, as a young bride. She'd promised herself she'd return 'one day.' Sixty-three years later, she kept that promise. Her stories about that 1957 voyage fascinated my teenagers in ways history books never could.

Glacier Bay became Great-Grandma's birthday venue. The naturalist announced her ninetieth birthday over the ship's PA. The entire dining room sang that evening. She cried. We all cried. A ninety-year-old in a wheelchair, surrounded by four generations she'd created, watching glaciers calve into blue water.

Statendam's size made group management possible. We could find each other at meals without formal arrangements. The kids developed their own social circles but remained within sight. The ship felt like a floating village where Hendersons lived.

My seven-year-old and my great-grandmother shared a moment in Ketchikan—holding hands while watching a bald eagle land in a spruce tree. Four generations apart in age, equal in wonder.

**The gathering:** Statendam carried four generations through Alaska together. The voyage that became our family's defining story.

Great-Grandma Ellen passed away two years later. But the photos from that voyage hang in every Henderson home. The celebration she'd waited sixty-three years to complete.

**The lesson:** Multi-generational travel works when the ship works harder than your family does.

stories.title: Married at Sea
stories.intended_reader: Couples considering romantic Alaska cruise experiences
stories.core_insight: Statendam's Alaska voyage combined honeymoon romance with nature's grandeur
stories.markdown:
**We didn't honeymoon on Statendam. We got married on her.**

The wedding industry overwhelmed us. Every decision created stress. Every vendor wanted more money. Six months of planning felt like six years of combat. Then my fiance had an idea: 'What if we skipped all this and got married in Alaska?'

[Statendam's](/ships/holland-america-line/statendam.html) captain married us on the bow as we entered Glacier Bay. Just us, the captain, two witnesses we'd met aboard, and eight hundred thousand acres of ice and mountain as backdrop. My wedding dress came from a shop in Juneau. My bouquet came from a Ketchikan florist. Everything we needed, Alaska provided.

The ceremony lasted twelve minutes. Calving glaciers provided the music. Ravens circled overhead. A humpback whale breached in the distance as we said 'I do.' The captain smiled. 'In forty years of conducting weddings, I've never had a whale as witness.'

Our honeymoon began immediately—no transition, no travel, no stress. Statendam's crew had arranged our stateroom with champagne and roses. The dining room captain presented a wedding cake the pastry chef had made overnight. Fellow passengers we'd never met toasted our marriage at dinner.

Every day brought new wonder. Kayaking in Sitka. Watching brown bears in Juneau. Dancing in the ship's ballroom while glaciers glowed pink in midnight sun. The honeymoon and the wedding merged into one continuous celebration.

We'd eloped to escape wedding stress. We discovered something better—a wedding that was pure joy, no obligation, no compromise. Alaska and Statendam gave us exactly what we needed.

**The beginning:** Statendam provided our wedding and honeymoon together. The cruise that started everything perfectly.

We celebrate our anniversary every year by watching videos from that day. The glacier, the whale, the captain's smile. The wedding we almost missed by planning something normal.

**The lesson:** The best honeymoon teaches partnership through both joy and challenge.

stories.title: The Alaska I Waited Eighty Years to See
stories.core_insight: Statendam's Alaska experience delivered a lifetime dream at a pace an eighty-year-old could manage
stories.markdown:
**Alaska had waited on my bucket list for sixty years. At eighty, I finally checked it off.**

My father worked on the Alaska Railroad in the 1930s. His stories filled my childhood—glaciers taller than buildings, bears fishing in streams, northern lights dancing overhead. I'd promised myself since age twenty that I'd see it someday. Someday kept retreating.

At eighty, my doctor offered a choice: 'Travel now or accept that some dreams won't happen.' I booked [Statendam](/ships/holland-america-line/statendam.html) the next day.

My body limited what I could do. Helicopter tours were out—my heart couldn't handle the altitude changes. Long hikes were impossible—my knees had retired years ago. But Statendam brought Alaska to me.

Glacier Bay unfolded from the comfort of a deck chair, wrapped in HAL's complimentary blanket, with a steward bringing hot cocoa without my asking. I saw icebergs the size of houses calve into cobalt water while sitting comfortably. No exertion required.

The ship's naturalist spent an hour with me personally, explaining what my father would have seen eighty years ago. How the glaciers had retreated. How the wildlife patterns had shifted. He helped me understand the Alaska my father knew and the Alaska I was witnessing.

Ketchikan worked perfectly—flat boardwalks I could manage, shops with places to sit, a pace that respected my limitations. Juneau offered wheelchair access to everything essential. The ship's shore excursion team had identified every tour that would work for me.

My father died in 1974. But watching the midnight sun from Statendam's deck, I felt him with me. 'I finally made it, Dad. You were right about everything.'

**The fulfillment:** Statendam let me fulfill a sixty-year-old promise. Alaska at eighty, exactly as I'd always imagined.

Some dreams wait until we're ready. Some destinations wait until we arrive. Alaska waited for me, and Statendam delivered me there.

**The lesson:** Age is irrelevant when service means respect.

stories.title: Finding My Husband in Glacier Bay
stories.intended_reader: Widows and widowers finding meaning through travel
stories.core_insight: Statendam's Alaska voyage helped a widow find peace with loss through nature's eternal perspective
stories.markdown:
**Thomas promised me Alaska for our fortieth anniversary. He died two months before we were supposed to go. I went anyway.**

The deposit was non-refundable—that's what I told myself when I didn't cancel. The truth was more complicated. Thomas had dreamed of Alaska for decades. He'd researched [Statendam](/ships/holland-america-line/statendam.html), chosen the cabin, planned the excursions. Canceling felt like losing him again.

The first day aboard was brutal. Every couple I saw reminded me of the couple we wouldn't become. Our anniversary cabin—upgraded to a suite, the cruise line's gesture of sympathy—felt enormous and empty. I nearly disembarked in Ketchikan and flew home.

Then Glacier Bay happened.

I stood on deck at five in the morning, unable to sleep, watching the Lamplugh Glacier approach. The ice had existed for thousands of years. It would exist for—who knows how long after. My grief, however consuming, was a moment in that glacier's existence. Thomas's death was a moment. Our marriage was a moment. The glacier didn't care.

That sounds cold. It wasn't. It was comforting. My loss was real but not unique. The world continued. Beauty continued. Alaska proved that existing things persisted despite tragedy.

A woman found me crying at the rail. She'd lost her wife three years before. We talked for an hour about grief and persistence and the strange comfort of ancient ice. She understood what Glacier Bay had given me: perspective.

**The peace:** Statendam carried me to a place that offered context for grief. The eternal ice that taught me loss wasn't the end.

I've been back to Alaska twice since. Each voyage brings Thomas with me in a different way. The dream he planned, fulfilled in his honor. The perspective he would have appreciated. The peace I found by going anyway.

**The lesson:** Grief needs beautiful places to breathe.

stories.title: Alaska from a Wheelchair
stories.intended_reader: Travelers with mobility disabilities considering Alaska cruises
stories.core_insight: Statendam's Alaska experience remained accessible to a passenger with significant mobility limitations
stories.markdown:
**Spinal cord injury at forty left me wheelchair-bound, and I spent twelve years believing Alaska was something that happened to other people—people who could stand at rails, walk forest trails, and climb onto whale-watching boats. [Statendam](/ships/holland-america-line/statendam.html) proved me wrong about all of it.**

My physical therapist suggested the cruise. 'The right ship brings the destination to you,' she said. I was skeptical. Alaska seemed designed for the able-bodied—the wilderness, the terrain, the adventure requiring legs that worked. But she had researched Statendam's accessibility, and her assessment convinced me to try.

I expected compromise. I received excellence. Statendam's accessibility coordinator spent two hours on the phone before I booked, explaining exactly what would and would not work. Honest assessment rather than sales optimism. She identified which shore excursions could accommodate wheelchairs, which could not, and which could with modification. She arranged a shower chair in my adapted cabin. She notified the crew.

Glacier Bay became my proving ground. I had worried I would miss the experience trapped inside the ship while able-bodied passengers crowded the outdoor decks. Instead, the crew positioned my wheelchair at the prime viewing spot on the forward deck, secured it safely against the ship's motion, and brought blankets and hot drinks as if I were the guest of honor. Rangers from the National Park Service came to my location to answer questions. The experience was not diminished. It was customized.

Juneau surprised me. The Mendenhall Glacier Visitor Center offered wheelchair-accessible trails with views of the glacier that rivaled any hiking trail. A whale-watching vessel with wheelchair access let me witness humpbacks breach from water level. Downtown Juneau, flat and tourist-friendly, allowed independent exploration.

Ketchikan's boardwalks worked perfectly—flat, well-maintained, wide enough for my chair. Sitka's waterfront welcomed me without obstacle. Even the tender operations, which I had feared most, went smoothly with crew members trained in wheelchair transfers who moved me between ship and boat with practiced efficiency.

I had believed that adventure ended with my injury. That Alaska was for other people. That wilderness required legs. Statendam taught me otherwise. The ship brought Alaska to my wheelchair, and Alaska proved that wonder does not require standing.

**The lesson:** Accessibility means equal dignity, not special treatment.

stories.title: Why I Finally Understood My Parents
stories.intended_reader: Cruise skeptics considering their first cruise
stories.core_insight: Statendam's Alaska voyage converted a cruise skeptic into a cruise enthusiast
stories.markdown:
**My parents had cruised for decades. I had spent those decades rolling my eyes. [Statendam](/ships/holland-america-line/statendam.html) proved them right and proved me spectacularly, humblingly, completely wrong.**

I thought cruises were for people who could not handle real travel. Floating buffets with shuffleboard. Tourists who wanted their adventures sanitized and their comforts guaranteed. I backpacked through Southeast Asia, trekked in Patagonia, camped in Mongolia. Real travel. Authentic travel. The kind of travel that cruises, I believed, existed to prevent.

My mother issued a challenge for her seventieth birthday: 'Take one cruise before you criticize. Statendam to Alaska. Then judge.' I agreed because refusing would have been ungracious and because, secretly, I wanted ammunition for my next critique.

The first two days, I searched for evidence of my prejudices. The buffet existed, but so did refined dining that challenged any restaurant I had visited in my adventure travels. The passengers were not all elderly—I met a documentary filmmaker, a retired astronaut, a marine biologist conducting ongoing research. The Alaska itinerary offered genuine adventure, not sanitized approximations.

Glacier Bay shattered my remaining skepticism. Statendam anchored in waters I could not have reached otherwise—not by car, barely by small plane, impossible on foot. The ship was not avoiding wilderness. She was accessing wilderness unavailable to landlocked travelers. My backpack could not have brought me here. Statendam could and did.

The naturalist programs impressed my pretentious soul. Real scientists explaining glaciology, marine biology, Alaska Native history with rigor that exceeded most adventure tours. I had expected simplified summaries. I received graduate-level education.

By Ketchikan, I understood what my parents had always known. Cruising was not cheating. It was efficient access to places that rewarded careful attention. The ship was not a bubble—it was a base camp. Statendam had done what my years of adventure travel never could: she placed me in the middle of genuine wilderness with the support to actually experience it.

I have cruised eight times since that voyage. I owe my parents an apology. And I owe Statendam gratitude for teaching me what I had been too arrogant to learn.

**The lesson:** First voyages teach what no brochure can—that cruising is less about the ship and more about who you become aboard her.

stories.title: Our Ruby Anniversary in Alaska
stories.core_insight: Statendam's intimate S-Class atmosphere and Alaska's grandeur created perfect anniversary celebration
stories.markdown:
**Forty years of marriage. We wanted glaciers, not ballrooms. Statendam delivered both.**

[Statendam](/ships/holland-america-line/statendam.html) became our ruby anniversary voyage—forty years together celebrated where nature provided the backdrop. We'd married in Alaska, eloping to Juneau in 1984 when my parents objected to a union they later embraced. Returning for our fortieth felt like coming full circle.

The ship knew our milestone. I'd mentioned it when booking, expecting perhaps a card. What unfolded exceeded any restaurant anniversary we could have imagined.

Our stateroom had been transformed. Forty roses—red, for ruby—arranged throughout the cabin. A bottle of champagne chilled. A card from the captain welcoming us back to Alaska for this milestone. Someone had researched that we'd married here. Someone had remembered.

The crew's attention accumulated throughout the voyage. Special desserts appearing at dinner with 'Happy 40th' inscribed. The bartender learning our wedding song and having the piano player perform it at sunset. Small gifts arriving daily—a framed photo of the ship, commemorative coins, a navigation chart of our route.

Juneau arrived on our actual anniversary date. The shore excursion team had arranged something impossible: a visit to the chapel where we'd married forty years earlier. A private moment in the building that had witnessed our beginning, followed by lunch at the same restaurant where we'd celebrated afterward—the restaurant that was somehow still there, somehow still serving halibut.

I watched my wife sit in the same pew where she'd become my wife four decades earlier. She was crying. I was crying. The minister who ran the chapel now—not the one who'd married us, he'd passed years ago—offered a blessing for our continued journey.

That evening, Statendam's dining room hosted our celebration dinner. The staff had gathered passengers who'd heard about our anniversary. Strangers who'd become friends raised glasses. The chef had prepared a replica of our wedding cake—somehow, my wife had mentioned it to our cabin steward days earlier, and he'd passed the information along.

**The return:** Statendam helped us celebrate where our marriage began. The ship that honored forty years by returning us home.

We've celebrated many anniversaries since. None matched that voyage—the culmination of four decades honored in the place where those decades started.

**The lesson:** Milestone celebrations gain meaning when shared with those who recognize what milestones cost.

stories.title: Alaska Rebuilt What Surgery Took
stories.intended_reader: Those recovering from major surgery seeking meaningful recovery experiences
stories.core_insight: Statendam's intimate scale and Alaska's restorative wilderness supported recovery from major medical procedures
stories.markdown:
**The hip replacement was supposed to give me back my life. Alaska actually did.**

[Statendam](/ships/holland-america-line/statendam.html) became my rehabilitation cruise—four months after hip replacement surgery, my first test of whether travel remained possible. The orthopedist had cleared me for 'gentle activity.' My physical therapist suggested a cruise: walking available, rest available, medical staff aboard. Alaska seemed ambitious. She said ambitious mattered.

I boarded uncertain. The new hip functioned but felt foreign. Walking happened but slowly. The gangway felt like a mountain. I questioned my judgment before I'd reached my cabin.

The crew noticed without announcement. My cabin steward positioned furniture for easier navigation. The dining room staff offered chairs with arms that made sitting and rising easier. The spa receptionist identified which treatments supported hip recovery. Nobody made me feel fragile. They simply removed obstacles.

The Promenade Deck became my therapy. Statendam's S-Class proportions meant I could complete circuits without exhaustion. Each day, I walked farther. Each day, the hip cooperated more. The crew learned to expect me at 7 AM, coffee waiting at my finishing spot.

Glacier Bay provided my breakthrough. I'd planned to watch from inside, uncertain about standing at the rail for hours. But when Margerie Glacier appeared, something moved me outside. I stood at that rail, weight on my new hip, watching ice older than any surgery calve into ancient water.

The cold helped—I hadn't expected that. The hip that ached in warm weather felt stable in Alaska's chill. The glacier's thunder matched something releasing in my body. I stood for two hours. No pain. No fear. Just wonder.

A fellow passenger noticed my careful movements. She'd had the same surgery three years earlier. 'It gets better,' she promised. 'My third year, I hiked Mount Kilimanjaro.' Her presence proved recovery continued beyond what I'd imagined.

Juneau offered accessible trails I hadn't known existed. Wheelchair-friendly paths with glacier views. Flat boardwalks through rainforest. Alaska adapted; my limitations didn't disqualify.

My final evening, I walked the Promenade Deck four times—twice my embarkation capacity. The hip that had failed me had been replaced. The body that had frightened me had begun rebuilding. Statendam had witnessed my recovery's acceleration.

**The rehabilitation:** Statendam gave my recovery what physical therapy couldn't—proof that the world still welcomed me. The ship that showed me mobility returned.

I've cruised six times since that voyage. Each one measures continued recovery. Each one confirms what Statendam first showed me: new hips still walk through beautiful places.

**The lesson:** Recovery requires beauty that asks nothing in return.
